title: Hieracium pseudelatum
taxonomy: Hieracium pseudelatum is classified within the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This plant is a member of the genus Hieracium, which includes approximately 1000 species of hawkweeds. The specific species, Hieracium pseudelatum, is a perennial herb native to the mountainous regions of Europe. It belongs to the subgenus Pilosella, which is characterized by its hairy leaves and stems. As a member of the Hieracium genus, Hieracium pseudelatum is a close relative of other plants in the same genus, such as Hieracium aurantiacum and Hieracium lachenalii.

constituents: Hieracium pseudelatum contains sesquiterpene lactones, such as pseudopilosulin, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. These compounds are known for their anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal activities. Additionally, Hieracium pseudelatum may also contain flavonoids and phenolic acids, which contribute to its pharmacological effects. The sesquiterpene lactones in Hieracium pseudelatum have been shown to have potential therapeutic applications, including treating skin conditions and infections. Further research is needed to fully understand the active constituents and potential uses of Hieracium pseudelatum.

Hieracium pseudelatum is a species in the dandelion family and is native to certain regions. As with many plants in this genus, Hieracium pseudelatum may cause skin irritation or allergic reactions in some individuals upon contact.
intro: Hieracium pseudelatum, commonly known as False Hawkweed, is a perennial plant in the dandelion family (Asteraceae) characterized by its yellow dandelion-like flowers and jagged leaf edges. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiviral effects, which may help alleviate symptoms of digestive issues, skin conditions, and respiratory problems. The bioactive constituents of Hieracium pseudelatum include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Hieracium pseudelatum include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which can be used internally to support overall health and well-being.
